test_set_source: exp-002 loop-optimization run (B / L7 / L8). Three questions, one per locked category; texts supplied verbatim by operator 2026-07-12.
status: READY — question texts locked verbatim by operator (frozen-question rule); categories + shape locked; output-format appended byte-identical to all arms.
design: 3 questions (one per category) x 3 arms (B, L7, L8) x 1 run = 9 answer-runs (3 per question)

arms.L8: v8 loop (v7 + OPT-1 composed context + Codex panel)

shared_materials: per-question; attach identical inputs to all three arms

categories.1: day-in-the-lab: ingest given publications -> analyse -> follow-up hypothesis + design test experiment
categories.2: maximum-creativity big-think: a big unanswered biological question -> generate + argue a genuinely new hypothesis
categories.3: translational data breakthrough: pull literature+datasets -> analyse -> predict + argue a new treatment

questions.category_name: day-in-the-lab: ingest given publications -> analyse -> follow-up hypothesis + design test experiment
questions.text: inspired by the publication attached, generate a novel method to identify a different metabolite cocktail that rejuvenates the skeletal muscle in vivo, what would be your first pilot experiment to test this prediction? (paper attached)
materials: resources/papers/1C Metabolite Cocktail Muscle Reprogramming.pdf  (S-083: Hernandez-Benitez et al. 2024, Cell Reports Medicine 5, 101449, DOI 10.1016/j.xcrm.2024.101449)
questions.fairness_rule: word-identical to all 3 arms; identical attached inputs; output-format appended byte-identical
questions.why_this_question: Category 1 (day-in-the-lab): ingest a given publication, then produce a NEW hypothesis/method FROM it plus a first pilot experiment. The 1C-metabolite paper (S-083) shows an in-vivo metabolite cocktail accelerates muscle regeneration in young + aged mice; the question asks for a novel METHOD to identify a DIFFERENT rejuvenating cocktail + a first pilot. Distinct topic from exp-001's ergothioneine/exercise-performance Q_ERGO, same shape (paper -> hypothesis + pilot). Note: this is a meta-level ask (a method to discover a cocktail), which stresses experimental-design creativity.

questions.category_name: maximum-creativity big-think: a big unanswered biological question -> generate + argue a genuinely new hypothesis
questions.text: Generate a novel, plausible, unifying hypothesis for: Why does biological aging occur?
questions.fairness_rule: word-identical to all 3 arms; output-format appended byte-identical
questions.why_this_question: Category 2 (maximum-creativity big-think): a big unanswered biological question -> generate + argue a genuinely new UNIFYING hypothesis, no materials, no expected experiment. 'Why does biological aging occur?' is a grand open question with many competing partial theories (damage/programmed/antagonistic-pleiotropy/disposable-soma/hyperfunction), so a genuinely unifying novel hypothesis is the maximum-creativity target. Distinct topic from exp-001's origin-of-life Q2, same shape (pure novel-hypothesis generation).

questions.category_name: translational data breakthrough: pull literature+datasets -> analyse -> predict + argue a new treatment
questions.text: Starting from publicly available datasets, propose a not-thought-of-yet or new compound to treat complex-I mitochondrial diseases.
questions.fairness_rule: word-identical to all 3 arms; output-format appended byte-identical
questions.why_this_question: Category 3 (translational data breakthrough): from publicly available datasets, analyse -> predict + argue a NEW compound to treat a disease. Complex-I mitochondrial diseases (e.g. Leigh syndrome, MELAS-spectrum) are a translational target with public multi-omics + chemical datasets and no approved disease-modifying drug, giving dataset-grounded novelty headroom. Distinct topic from exp-001's sarcopenia exercise-mimetic Q3, same shape (public datasets -> novel compound for a disease).

output_format_instruction: How to present your results. Please hand back your answer as a small presentation folder containing: result.md — open with a "Bottom line" box of 150 words or fewer (your single main answer, and — if you propose one — the first experiment, in plain language a busy reader grasps in 30 seconds), then give your full answer and the case for it. Keep result.md to about 1,800 words or fewer (about 4 pages), excluding figures and the bottom-line box — be complete but concise; length earns no credit, clarity does. reasoning.md — the line of thought that got you there, written as structured bullet points or short sections (not long prose); no length limit, but keep it scannable. a figures/ folder with any plots, tables, or other artefacts you produced, each clearly labelled; sources.md — every source you drew on, with identifiers; and process_trace.json — a step-by-step record of what you actually did to reach the answer.
output_format_rule: THE delivered output-format string (plain-prose), appended byte-identical to EVERY question and EVERY arm (B/L7/L8) as delivered_prompt = question.text + '\n\n' + output_format_instruction. Wording identical to loop-design/current/output-format-instruction.md. Carries the exp-002 update: ~1,800-word cap on result.md + mandatory <=150-word bottom-line box + bulleted reasoning.md. Format lives in the question, never engineered into the loop.
